Surprise
Identify all of the potential surprises in the story. Every story has them.
Determine the information that the audience requires in order for the surprise to
pay off. What do you need to teach the audience in order for them to be surprised?
Preserve and protect that information by hiding it from the audience-via the
following strategies:
Give the information a secondary reason for being included in the story (if that
reason doesn’t already exist)
Hide it in a list of other things to minimize its importance
Make it funny
Determine the order or words that maximizes the surprise
In order to maximize surprise in your personal story, follow these steps:

2.
3.
Surprise is almost always the reason an audience laughs, cries, or feels any other
meaningful emotion. It’s why it’s so critical to get it right.
Once you’ve been surprised, it’s hard to remember being surprised, so you forget
that your audience should be surprised.
Surprises are often predicated on providing the audience some just enough
information for the surprise to pay off. Storytellers often provide too much
information or fail to marginalize the information needed so that the audience sees
the surprise long before it arrives.
Surprise requires planning. It’s often predicated on the order of words that you
speak. This level of planning is often ignored by storytellers.
Surprise is the most delightful thing that a storyteller has to offer their audience. It's
unforgettable and often produces the largest emotional responses.
Surprise is also the part of stories that storytellers most frequently botch. The
problems are many:
1.
2.
3.
This lesson will help you identify, preserve, and enhance surprise in the stories you tell.
